summary: Kenneth argues that cash-secured puts don't always make sense as part of the wheel strategy, using Joby Aviation as a case study where poor premium-to-capital-ratio and unfavorable risk/reward profiles make alternative capital deployment more efficient for income generation.
takeaways: Don't sell cash-secured puts when premium-to-capital ratio is poor (e.g., $3,600 capital for $10-20 premium)
Use Fibonacci retracements to identify potential downside targets before committing to cash-secured puts
If selling 2 cash-secured puts at a strike yields only $90 premium on $3,600 capital, redeploy that capital to covered calls on higher-premium opportunities
Avoid cash-secured puts on stocks with massive runups (high volatility/retracement risk) when premium doesn't justify the capital lockup
Time the market: wait for stocks to drop to Fibonacci support levels, then buy shares and sell covered calls instead of selling puts preemptively
Premium threshold rule: $90 on $3,600 (2.5% return) is not a good use of capital—seek higher-return opportunities

strategy_candidates.change: Add capital-efficiency filter: require minimum 3%+ monthly return (in line with $1,250/mo target on $50k = 2.5%) before deploying capital to cash-secured puts; otherwise redeploy to covered calls on stocks in Fibonacci support zones
strategy_candidates.evidence: Kenneth explicitly rejected $90 premium on $3,600 capital (2.5% return) for JOBY and showed buying at $15.51 support + selling Friday covered calls yields 'way more money' than pre-assigned puts

title: Why cash secured puts aren’t always an option
